skill.name: spawn-agent
skill.description: Spawn supported AI coding agents in a new terminal, including Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, OpenCode, and Copilot.
skill.summary: Launch configured AI coding agents in separate terminals with cookbook guidance for each CLI.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command execution indicators. Review confirms the core implementation launches caller-controlled shell commands and the cookbooks recommend approval-bypass modes for several AI CLIs. No confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection was found, but the skill has high operational risk and should not be published without strong warnings and safeguards.

high_findings.title: Unrestricted Shell Command Launching
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: fork_terminal accepts a command string and interpolates it into Terminal, PowerShell, WSL, tmux, and shell=True execution paths. This is the intended feature, but any unsafe prompt-to-command construction can execute arbitrary local commands.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly constructs shell commands from the command parameter and uses subprocess or terminal launchers. The behavior is central to the skill, so the risk is confirmed even though legitimate use is possible.

high_findings.title: Default Guidance Disables Agent Safety Controls
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: multiple cookbooks instruct agents to use permission bypass, full-auto, yolo, force, or allow-all-tools modes. These settings can let spawned agents edit files and run commands with little or no user confirmation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cookbook text explicitly recommends broad auto-approval flags. The commands are documented instructions, not merely examples unrelated to runtime behavior.

medium_findings.title: Agent Prompts and Outputs Written to Temporary Files
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: the implementation writes agent output, debug output, done markers, and generated WSL scripts under /tmp. Captured prompts or agent output may contain sensitive project data and can remain readable after the run depending on host permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and writes are explicit, and the prompt template shows conversation summaries are intended content. Exposure depends on local filesystem permissions, so severity is medium.

medium_findings.title: Provider Authentication Flow Opens External CLI Sessions
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: fork_for_auth launches provider login commands in a terminal and waits for completion. This is useful for recovery, but users should understand that credentials and tokens are handled by external CLIs outside this skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The login commands are fixed and provider-scoped, which reduces injection risk. The risk is mainly delegated credential handling and external session behavior.

low_findings.title: Static False Positives in Markdown and Identifier Text
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: many weak-cryptography, Ruby backtick, key-file, obfuscation, and reconnaissance hits come from Markdown examples, headings, file extensions, dictionary key text, or command reference tables. I did not find evidence of actual cryptographic code or obfuscated payloads in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review showed these cited lines are documentation or benign identifiers, not crypto or credential access. The overall skill remains risky due to real command execution elsewhere.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance and Network Hits Are Mostly Contextual
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: /proc access is used to detect WSL, and OpenCode hostname and server references are documented CLI options. These are not malicious by themselves, but they add context to a tool that can launch networked AI CLIs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed code uses /proc/version only for platform detection, and the network references are command documentation. No evidence found of hidden exfiltration endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: shell=True With Caller-Controlled Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The Linux fallback passes the command string to subprocess.Popen with shell=True. This pattern can execute injected shell metacharacters if command construction is not strictly controlled.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact shell=True calls are visible and use the command parameter. This is a direct command injection hazard if upstream prompt handling is unsafe.

dangerous_patterns.title: Interpolated Commands in Terminal Launchers
dangerous_patterns.description: The macOS, Windows, WSL, and tmux paths build executable command strings with interpolation. Quoting is incomplete for command and several generated file paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Command interpolation is visible across platform branches. Some paths avoid shell=True, but the launched shell still interprets composed command text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Approval Bypass Flags in Spawned Agents
dangerous_patterns.description: The cookbooks instruct use of high-trust execution flags such as dangerously-skip-permissions, dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox, yolo, force, and allow-all-tools.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky flags are explicitly recommended in command construction guidance. Their effect is to reduce user approval barriers for spawned agents.

content.user_title: Spawn AI Coding Agents in New Terminals
content.value_statement: Developers often switch between AI coding CLIs and need a repeatable way to launch them. This skill provides cookbook-driven terminal spawning for supported agents with output capture options.
actual_capabilities: Selects a supported AI coding agent from explicit user requests or a default setting.
Provides cookbook guidance for Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, OpenCode, and Copilot CLIs.
Launches agent commands in a new terminal window, tmux window, WSL session, or fallback shell process.
Can capture clean agent output or debug terminal output into temporary files.
Includes a summary prompt template for handing conversation context to a spawned agent.
Provides helper logic for detecting common authentication failure messages.
limitations: It depends on local installation and authentication for each external AI CLI.
It can run powerful local commands and should be used only in trusted workspaces.
Output capture writes temporary files that may include sensitive project context.
Platform behavior differs across macOS, Windows, WSL, Linux, and tmux.

use_cases.title: Launch a Secondary Coding Agent
use_cases.description: Start a separate Claude Code, Codex, or Gemini session to investigate a task while the main session continues.

use_cases.title: Compare Agent Responses
use_cases.description: Run the same task through different coding CLIs and compare their plans, summaries, or implementation suggestions.

use_cases.title: Recover Authentication Interactively
use_cases.description: Open a terminal-based login flow when an AI CLI needs user authentication before continuing work.

prompt_templates.title: Start Default Agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request using the default Claude Code behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Spawn an agent to inspect the failing tests and report the likely cause.

prompt_templates.title: Use a Specific Agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose one supported CLI for a focused coding task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Spawn Codex to review the authentication module and summarize any risky assumptions.

prompt_templates.title: Pass Conversation Context
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a handoff when another terminal agent needs recent context.
prompt_templates.prompt: Spawn Gemini with a summary of our conversation and ask it to propose a migration plan.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Agents
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for cross-checking a complex engineering decision.
prompt_templates.prompt: Spawn Cursor and OpenCode in separate terminals to evaluate this refactor plan and identify incompatible assumptions.

output_examples.input: Spawn Claude Code to inspect the API client.
output_examples.output: A new terminal opens with Claude Code in the selected workspace. The agent output path is returned for later review.

output_examples.input: Spawn Codex with a summary and capture output.
output_examples.output: The skill prepares a summary prompt, launches Codex, and stores the clean response in a temporary agent output file.

output_examples.input: Use Gemini fast model to check documentation gaps.
output_examples.output: A Gemini CLI session starts with the requested model routing and the documentation review prompt.

best_practices: Use this skill only in repositories where terminal command execution is acceptable.
Review the selected cookbook and CLI help output before launching the agent.
Delete captured temporary output when it may contain secrets or private project details.
anti_patterns: Do not spawn agents with sensitive credentials or private keys in the prompt.
Do not use approval-bypass modes in untrusted repositories or shared machines.
Do not assume every supported CLI has the same sandbox or output behavior.

faq.question: Which agents can this skill launch?
faq.answer: It documents flows for Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Cursor, OpenCode, and GitHub Copilot CLI.

faq.question: Does it install the AI CLIs?
faq.answer: No. The required CLI tools must already be installed and authenticated on the local machine.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The skill launches shell commands and recommends broad approval-bypass modes for several coding agents.

faq.question: Can it capture agent output?
faq.answer: Yes. It can write clean agent output or debug terminal output to temporary files.

faq.question: Does it work on every platform?
faq.answer: It includes branches for macOS, Windows, WSL, Linux with tmux, and a Linux fallback.

faq.question: Should captured files be kept?
faq.answer: Keep them only when needed. Remove them if they include private prompts, code, or agent responses.
